chunk_summary: Risk-stratified follow-up for MAM involves baseline assessment, risk-based scheduling, and response evaluation to ensure early intervention and program success, with WHO criteria guiding intensity and trajectory monitoring.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of structured baseline assessments in MAM follow-up care.
Learn to classify children into high-risk and standard-risk categories using WHO criteria.
Develop skills to assess response trajectories and adjust follow-up plans accordingly.
Familiarize with state models for follow-up scheduling in outpatient MAM programs.
key_facts: Baseline assessment includes MUAC, WAZ, and dietary/health drivers documentation.
WHO risk criteria include MUAC 115–119 mm, WAZ < -3, age < 24 months, relapse, comorbidity, or severe social adversity.
High-risk children should be reviewed within 7 days, while standard-risk children within 14 days.
Follow-up focuses on trajectory: clinical well-being, improved eating, and consistent anthropometric gains.
State models often recommend weekly or fortnightly visits during an 8-week active program period.
reference_quote: Start with a structured day 0 plan: document baseline anthropometry (weight, length/height when feasible, oedema, and at least one of MUAC or WHZ/WLZ), note which metric defined eligibility, identify dietary and health drivers, and link the child to a reliable supplementary feeding pathway. (WHO, 2023)
document_key_topics: State models for outpatient MAM care
